case: C.R No. 1238-P of 2025 Mian Sohrab Gul Vs Muneer & others
remarks:
Right to lawful trade, profession, occupation, business and issuance of No Objection Certificate by the Authority.
The entire claim of the petitioner is founded upon the assumption that issuance of an NOC in his favour vested him with an exclusive right to carry on the business of a fruit and vegetable market and simultaneously disabled the municipal authorities from granting similar permissions to others. Such contention is legally untenable. The right to engage in lawful trade, profession, occupation or business is a constitutionally protected right. The freedom to carry on trade and business cannot be curtailed except in accordance with law and for reasons sanctioned by the Constitution. Regulatory authorities may impose reasonable conditions in the public interest concerning licensing, hygiene, traffic management, environmental concerns, municipal planning and public safety; however, such regulation cannot ordinarily be transformed into a mechanism for creating monopolies in favour of private individuals unless expressly authorized by law. The powers vested in Tehsil Municipal Administrations are regulatory and supervisory in character. Their function is to regulate municipal affairs, maintain public order, ensure civic convenience, manage traffic concerns, enforce by-laws and facilitate lawful commercial activities within their jurisdiction. These powers cannot be construed as enabling the conferment of exclusive commercial privileges upon one trader to the exclusion of all others unless such exclusivity is specifically sanctioned by statute. No such statutory foundation has been pointed out in the present case.

case: RFA No. 12/2024 Muhammad Ijaz Vs Zia-Ul-Haq
remarks:
Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (XXVI of 1881)—Sections 6, 72 & 84—Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (V of 1908)—Order XXXVII—Suit based on cheque—Presentment of cheque—Cause of action—A cheque is a bill of exchange payable on demand. Presentment of the cheque before the drawee bank within a reasonable time is an essential condition precedent for accrual of a cause of action under Order XXXVII, C.P.C. Unless the cheque is presented and payment is refused, no legal cause of action matures for maintaining a summary suit founded upon such cheque. Mere execution or possession of the cheque does not dispense with proof of its presentment and dishonour.—Paras 4 to 7.
Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (XXVI of 1881)—Sections 72 & 84—Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (V of 1908)—Order XXXVII—Suit for recovery on cheque—Burden of proof—Where the plaintiff failed to produce any cheque return memo, dishonour slip, bank certificate or other reliable banking record establishing that the cheque had been presented and dishonoured, the statutory presumption attached to a negotiable instrument could not substitute proof of those foundational facts which alone give rise to the cause of action.—Paras 7 & 9.
Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (XXVI of 1881)—Section 84—Cheque—Stale cheque—Reasonable time for presentment—A cheque issued in the year 2015 but made the basis of a summary suit instituted more than two years later, without proof of its presentation within the ordinary banking validity period, had become stale. Banking usage consistently recognizes that a cheque not presented within six months ordinarily ceases to be enforceable through normal banking channels unless revalidated by the drawer. Such a cheque cannot furnish a valid basis for a decree under Order XXXVII, C.P.C.—Para 8.
Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (V of 1908)—Order XXXVII—Summary jurisdiction—Nature and scope—Summary procedure is an exceptional remedy which curtails the ordinary right of defence. A plaintiff seeking to invoke such jurisdiction must strictly establish compliance with all statutory requirements governing the negotiable instrument. An underlying business transaction, by itself, cannot validate a cheque which was neither presented nor dishonoured, nor can it sustain a decree under the summary procedure.—Paras 9 & 10.
Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 (V of 1908)—Order XXXVII—Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (XXVI of 1881)—Sections 72 & 84—Summary suit—Cheque neither presented nor dishonoured—Cheque having become stale—Trial Court decreed the suit merely on the statutory presumption attached to the cheque—Validity—Held, where the cheque was not proved to have been duly presented and dishonoured and had become stale long before institution of the suit, it could not furnish a valid basis for passing a decree under Order XXXVII, C.P.C. Judgment and decree set aside in appeal and suit dismissed.—Paras 11 & 12.

case: C.R No. 850-P of 2026 Muhammad Zeeshan Ud Din Vs Mst. Rukhsana Shafique & another
remarks:
Section 35 & 35-A read with Order XLI rule 33 CPC.
• The Code of Civil Procedure draws a clear distinction between ordinary costs under Section 35, C.P.C. and compensatory costs under Section 35-A, C.P.C. While Section 35 confers a discretionary power upon the Court to award ordinary costs of litigation with a view to indemnifying the successful party for expenses reasonably incurred in prosecuting or defending the proceedings, Section 35-A creates a special and penal jurisdiction enabling the Court to award compensatory costs only where the claim or defence is found to be false or vexatious to the knowledge of the party raising it and the statutory conditions prescribed therein have been strictly fulfilled. More importantly, the jurisdiction to award compensatory costs under Section 35-A, C.P.C. is vested exclusively in the Court of first instance trying the suit. The appellate Court does not possess an independent or concurrent jurisdiction to exercise that power. This legal position is placed beyond doubt by the proviso to Order XLI, Rule 33, C.P.C., which expressly restrains an appellate Court from making any order under Section 35-A in respect of an objection upon which the trial Court has omitted or refused to pass such an order. Although Rule 33 confers wide discretionary powers upon an appellate Court to pass any decree or order which ought to have been passed by the Court below, the proviso engrafts a specific statutory limitation by excluding the jurisdiction to award compensatory costs under Section 35-A, C.P.C. Consequently, while an appellate Court may affirm, modify or set aside ordinary costs awarded under Section 35, C.P.C., it is denuded of jurisdiction to independently impose compensatory costs.
• In the present case, the learned appellate Court rightly concluded that the plaint could not have been rejected under Order VII, Rule 11, C.P.C., as the controversy between the parties involved disputed and intricate questions of fact and law requiring determination after framing of issues and recording of evidence. The order of remand, therefore, suffers from no legal infirmity and calls for no interference. However, while allowing the appeal, the learned appellate Court further burdened the petitioner with costs of Rs. 50,000/-. The nature and tenor of the order unmistakably demonstrate that the amount was imposed as compensatory costs and not as ordinary costs under Section 35, C.P.C. Such an exercise of jurisdiction is expressly prohibited by the proviso to Order XLI, Rule 33, C.P.C. and is, therefore, without lawful authority. Accordingly, this civil revision fails on merits insofar as the remand of the suit for trial is concerned, as the learned appellate Court rightly held that the controversy could not be adjudicated within the limited scope of Order VII, Rule 11, C.P.C. and necessarily required a full-fledged trial. However, the direction imposing costs of Rs. 50,000/- being beyond the jurisdiction vested in the appellate Court under Section 35-A read with the proviso to Order XLI, Rule 33, C.P.C., is declared illegal and is hereby set aside.

case: Cr.A No. 1216-P of 2025 Habib Ullah Vs The State
remarks:
(a) Section 302,109/34 Pakistan Penal Code- Circumstantial Evidence – Standard of Proof
Held- For proving the guilt, it must be examined whether the circumstances proved by the prosecution form such a complete and unbroken chain as to irresistibly point towards the guilt of the appellant and exclude every reasonable hypothesis consistent with his innocence..
(b) Article 122, Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984 – Facts within Knowledge in Matrimonial Home
Held- The occurrence having taken place within the confines of the matrimonial home, the appellant was the person best placed to explain the circumstances under which his wife suffered the fatal injuries. Although the burden of proving the charge always remains upon the prosecution, yet where certain facts are especially within the knowledge of an accused, the law embodied in Article 122 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, casts upon him the obligation of offering a plausible explanation regarding such facts.
Ref: Saeed Ahmed v. The State (2015 SCMR 710), Waris Masih v. The State (Jail Petition No.787 of 2018).
(c) Section 302,109/34 Pakistan Penal Code- Unnatural Death inside House – Effect of Failure to Explain
Held- where an unnatural death occurs within a house and the attending circumstances are especially within the knowledge of the accused, his failure to furnish a satisfactory explanation constitutes an additional link in the chain of incriminating circumstances. ... His silence in the face of such a grave circumstance is not without legal consequence and, in the peculiar facts of the present case, constitutes an additional incriminating circumstance against him.
Ref: Saeed Ahmed v. The State (2015 SCMR 710).
(d) Abscondence – Evidentiary Value
Held- Although abscondence by itself is not conclusive proof of guilt, yet it remains a relevant circumstance which can legitimately be taken into consideration when examined in conjunction with the other evidence available on the record. In the present case, the conduct of the appellant in avoiding arrest immediately after the occurrence lends corroboration to the prosecution case.
(e) Motive – Status when Guilt is Independently Established
Held- It is by now well settled that motive is merely a corroborative circumstance and where the remaining evidence independently establishes the guilt of an accused, failure to prove motive is not fatal.
The conviction and sentence recorded by the learned trial Court are maintained and the appeal is dismissed,

case: Cr.A No. 641-P of 2025 Sharafat Kazmi Vs The State
remarks:
(a) Section 9(d) - the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Control of Narcotic Substances Act, 2019- Evidence of Official Witnesses & Absence of Private Witnesses
Held- The law is now well settled that evidence cannot be discarded merely because it emanates from police or Excise officials. Their testimony is to be evaluated on the same touchstone as that of any other witness. Where such evidence is found trustworthy, confidence- inspiring and free from any ulterior motive, conviction can safely be founded thereon even in the absence of independent witnesses.
(b) Section 9(d) - the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Control of Narcotic Substances Act, 2019- Conscious Possession & Burden of Proof
Held- The appellant was the sole occupant and driver of the vehicle from whose luggage compartment the narcotics were recovered. The vehicle remained in his exclusive control and possession. In such circumstances, conscious possession stands fully established. Once exclusive possession is proved, the burden shifts upon the accused to furnish a plausible explanation regarding the presence of the contraband.
(c) Section 9(d) - the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Control of Narcotic Substances Act, 2019- Forensic Evidence & Safe Custody
Held- The report of the Forensic Science Laboratory constitutes strong corroborative scientific evidence. The report unequivocally confirms that the parcels received by the laboratory were duly sealed, the seals remained intact and tallied with the specimen impression, and the samples were found to contain charas. The evidentiary value of a positive FSL report, read with the unimpeached evidence regarding safe custody of the case property, lends complete assurance to the prosecution version.
(d) Section 9(d) - the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Control of Narcotic Substances Act, 2019- Minor Discrepancies & Variation in Weight
Held- The settled principle of law is that minor discrepancies, attributable to lapse of memory or normal errors of observation, are the hallmarks of truthful testimony and do not diminish the evidentiary value of otherwise reliable witnesses. ... The alleged difference is insignificant and stands reasonably explained by the natural process of evaporation, drying and the passage of time. Such minor variation neither affects the identity of the recovered substance nor casts any doubt upon the prosecution case.
(e) Section 9(d) - the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Control of Narcotic Substances Act, 2019- Defects in Investigation
Held- It is equally settled that defects or irregularities, if any, in the investigation do not by themselves furnish a ground for acquittal unless it is shown that such defects have caused serious prejudice to the accused or have rendered the prosecution case doubtful. The conviction and sentence awarded to the appellant are affirmed and the appeal is dismissed.

case: RFA No. 109-P of 2025 Ayaz Ahmed Paracha Vs Dost Muhammad Khan
remarks:
1. The legislature, being mindful of the practical realities of commercial dealings and the need for certainty in financial transactions, consciously enacted the provisions of Order XXXVII CPC, so that obligations evidenced through written acknowledgments and negotiable instruments may be enforced with promptitude and the agonies of protracted litigation may come to an end at the earliest possible stage.
2. The essence of jurisdiction under this provision is not defeated merely because, during the subsistence of commercial dealings, certain amounts were received, adjusted, or accounted for inter se the parties. What is legally material, is whether the liability, as finally crystallized, stands acknowledged in a definite quantified form through written acknowledgment and is further reinforced by issuance of negotiable instruments.
3. The controversy before the Court did not arise in a vacuum nor did it rest upon an isolated or disputed cash transaction rather, it emanated from an admitted business relationship extending over a period of time, during which financial dealings admittedly took place between the parties.
4. Such rebuttal requires more than a mere ‘bald assertion’ or a ‘moonshine’ defense; it necessitates the production of ‘independent and convincing evidence’ to show the absence of consideration. This Court is mindful that in commercial transactions parties often make periodic payments, receive profits, adjust accounts, or settle part of their liabilities from time to time however, such adjustments do not necessarily obliterate the principal liability where the parties themselves subsequently reduce their obligations into a written and quantified acknowledgment.

case: W.P No. 10023-P of 2025 Mian Junaid Sardar Vs Govt
remarks:
As regards the contention, vehemently advanced by the petitioners, that the marks awarded in the interview ought to have been separately allocated by each member of the Interview Panel and disclosed to the candidates, the same requires examination in the light of the governing statutory framework. The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Service Commission was established under Article 2422 of the Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973, and is entrusted with such functions as may be prescribed by law. Pursuant thereto, the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Service Commission Ordinance, 1978 (“Ordinance”) was enacted, which establishes the Commission as an independent statutory body responsible for conducting examinations and interviews for recruitment to public service. Sections 7, 10 and 10A of the Ordinance respectively define the functions of the Commission and empower the Government to frame rules and the Commission to make regulations for carrying out the purposes of the Ordinance. Consequently, the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Service Commission (Functions) Rules, 1983 and the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Public Service Commission Regulations, 2017 have been framed to regulate the recruitment process. In the context of the present controversy, Regulation 32 assumes significance.
“32. MERIT LIST. (a) When all the eligible candidates called for interview for particular post have been interviewed, the Director Recruitment, Director Examination or any other authorized officer, as the case may be, shall prepare the merit list as per the marks recorded on the evaluation sheet and submit the case for the Commission’s approval. (b) Merit list shall be prepared keeping in view the aggregate marks secured by all the qualified candidates. (c) When two or more than two candidates have secured equal marks in aggregate the candidate who has secured higher marks in the interview shall be marked senior. In case the marks in aggregate and also the marks in the interview are the same, the candidate who is older in age shall be placed senior to the one who is younger. In case all the above-mentioned factors are the same, marks obtained in the final examination of the prescribed qualification for the post shall be the deciding factor. (d) In the case of Competitive Examination the merit list shall be prepared in accordance with the laid down provisions of the syllabus of the respective service.”
A bare reading of Regulation 32 clarifies that, in the case of a Competitive Examination, the merit list is to be prepared in accordance with the provisions of the syllabus governing the respective service. More importantly, clauses (a) and (b) of Regulation 32 contemplate two distinct stages. Under clause (a), once all eligible candidates called for interview have been interviewed, a merit list is prepared on the basis of the marks recorded on the evaluation sheet and is submitted to the Commission for approval. Under clause (b), the merit list is thereafter prepared with reference to the aggregate marks secured by all qualified candidates. The distinction between the two clauses indicates that the evaluation sheet and the initial merit list encompass all interviewed candidates, whether successful or unsuccessful, whereas the final merit list under clause.
(b) relates only to those candidates who qualify the prescribed criteria. It, therefore, follows that even unsuccessful candidates form part of the evaluation process and have a legitimate interest in knowing the marks obtained by them in the interview as well as their overall position in the assessment process. This Court has already observed in Mst. Nabila Hidayat Ullah and Ms. Tehmeena Bibi3 that, in order to promote transparency and public confidence in the recruitment process, it would be appropriate that the marks awarded by each member of the Interview Panel be separately recorded and preserved as part of the official record, so that no room is left for manipulation or doubt. It has further been observed that even a candidate who fails in the interview should be informed of the marks obtained by him or her. Such an approach is also in consonance with the spirit of Article 19A4 of the Constitution of Pakistan, which guarantees the right of access to information in matters of public importance, subject to law.

case: RFA No. 263-A of 2023 Shahid Gul and another Vs Ziaullah Wazir and another
remarks:
Contract Act, 1872 (IX of 1872) — Sections 128, 129, 130 & 133-139 — Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 — Section 145 — Execution proceedings — Surety bond furnished for satisfaction of decretal amount — Application for withdrawal of surety — Scope — Distinction between continuing guarantee and decree-specific surety.
The undertaking furnished by the sureties was not a continuing guarantee within the contemplation of Sections 129 and 130 of the Contract Act, 1872. It did not relate to a series of future transactions but was furnished in relation to a specific and existing liability arising from a decree already passed by a competent Court. The liability was fixed, quantified and judicially determined. Consequently, Section 130 of the Contract Act had no application and the sureties could not unilaterally revoke or withdraw their undertaking. Sections 133 to 139 of the Contract Act enumerate the recognized grounds for discharge of a surety, but no provision confers an unrestricted right upon a surety to withdraw from a guarantee after it has been accepted and acted upon.
(Paras 14, 15, 16 & 17)
Contract Act, 1872 (IX of 1872) — Section 128 — Liability of surety — Co-extensive liability — Principal debtor and surety jointly and severally liable.
Section 128 of the Contract Act expressly provides that the liability of a surety is co-extensive with that of the principal debtor unless otherwise provided by the contract. Once the appellants voluntarily undertook before the Executing Court that they would satisfy the decretal amount in the event of default by the judgment-debtor, their liability became co-extensive with that of the judgment-debtor himself. The decree-holder was not legally required to first exhaust every possible remedy against the judgment-debtor before seeking enforcement of the surety bond. (Paras 12 & 13) Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 — Section 145 — Surety furnished before Court — Enforcement of liability — Withdrawal of surety — No statutory right.
Section 145, C.P.C. recognizes and enforces liabilities undertaken by sureties before Courts. The provision is founded upon the principle that a person who voluntarily undertakes responsibility before a Court becomes answerable in the same proceedings without compelling the successful litigant to institute fresh litigation. Neither Section 145, C.P.C. nor any other provision of the Code confers a right upon a surety, who has undertaken responsibility for satisfaction of a decree, to unilaterally withdraw from such undertaking after it has been accepted by the Court.
(Paras 18 & 19)
Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 — Order XXXVIII Rule 3 — Discharge of surety — Legislative intent. Order XXXVIII Rule 3, C.P.C. contains an express provision regarding discharge of a surety furnished for appearance. The existence of such a provision demonstrates that whenever the legislature intended to permit discharge of a surety, it did so expressly. No analogous provision exists in respect of a surety who undertakes responsibility for payment of decretal amount. The omission is deliberate and significant.
(Para 20)
Execution proceedings — Surety bond — Judicial undertaking — Withdrawal after acceptance by Court — Not permissible.
A surety who voluntarily furnishes an undertaking before a Court cannot subsequently wriggle out of the obligation assumed by him. If sureties are permitted to withdraw at will after securing the benefit for which the surety was furnished, the entire object of obtaining sureties in judicial proceedings would stand frustrated. Such a course would undermine confidence in judicial undertakings and seriously prejudice decree-holders who act upon such assurances. A surety who undertakes before a Court to satisfy a decree in the event of default by the judgment-debtor does not possess an independent statutory right to withdraw from that undertaking merely at his own election.
(Paras 21, 22 & 23)
Execution of decree — Surety — Effect of subsequent events — Discharge of liability. The broader principle emerging from the jurisprudence is that once liability has attached under a valid surety bond, it survives unless law or contract provides otherwise. Subsequent events do not ordinarily discharge a surety once liability has attached. No provision of law or term of the surety bond was shown which could absolve the appellants from the liability voluntarily undertaken by them before the Executing Court.
(Paras 24 & 25)
Execution proceedings — Decree-holder — Fruits of decree — Protection of rights. The decree-holder cannot be deprived of the fruits of a decree by technical pleas raised by persons who themselves voluntarily stepped into the execution proceedings and assumed responsibility for the judgment-debtor. Judicial proceedings rest upon confidence that assurances given to the Court will be honoured. A person who voluntarily assumes liability before a Court cannot ordinarily be permitted to retreat from such obligation once the Court and the opposite party have acted upon it.
(Paras 27 & 28)
Civil Procedure — Execution proceedings — Surety furnished for satisfaction of decree — Judicial surety distinguished from continuing commercial guarantee.
A judicial surety furnished for satisfaction of a decree stands on a fundamentally different footing from a continuing commercial guarantee and cannot be revoked unilaterally after acceptance by the Court. Neither the Contract Act, 1872 nor the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908 confers upon such sureties a right to withdraw from their undertaking after it has been accepted and acted upon by the Court.
(Para 32)

case: Cr.A No. 345-P of 2026 Qamar Zaman S.I Vs The State
remarks:
This view has consistently been affirmed by the superior Courts in numerous judgments. In Asif Atta v. The State (2025 PCr.LJ Lahore 1730), the Honourable Lahore High Court, while setting aside the conviction under Section 174 PPC, held as follows:
8. All offences in bracket of section 172‐188 P.P.C. shall ordinarily be tried under chapter XX of Cr.P.C., on a complaint, if the Judge or Magistrate does not attend offences under sections 175, 178, 179 and 180 of P.P.C. within the purview of procedure prescribed under section 480 of Cr.P.C; but when such offences are committed before himself or in contempt of his lawful authority, or is brought under his notice, as such Judge or Magistrate (except Judge of High Court) in the course of a judicial proceeding, he shall not try it himself. This command of law is incorporated in section 487 of Cr.P.C. which is as under;
487. Certain Judges and Magistrates not to try offences referred to in Section 195 when committed before themselves: (1) Except as provided in Sections 476, 480 and 485 no Judge of a Criminal Court or Magistrate, other than a Judge of a High Court, shall try any person for any offence referred to in Section 195, when such offence is committed before himself or in contempt of his authority, or is brought under his notice, as such Judge or Magistrate in the course of a judicial proceeding.
In the present case learned Additional Sessions Judge has sentenced the appellant under section 174 of P.P.C. for his failure to produce the accused against whom notice was issued on petition for cancellation of his bail. Section 174 falls in category of offences mentioned in section 195, subsection (1), clause (a), therefore, learned Additional Sessions Judge was not authorized to sentence the appellant by himself, rather complaint should have been forwarded to the Magistrate having jurisdiction in the matter, and on receiving such complaint concerned Magistrate is not required to record statement of Judge as mentioned in section 200 of Cr.P.C., rather follow the process contained in sections 200 to 204 Cr.P.C., and provisions relating to trial as the case may be.
The crux of the above‐referred judgment is that an offence under Section 174 PPC falls within the ambit of Section 195(1)(a Cr.P.C. Where such offence is committed before a Judge or Magistrate, or in contempt of his authority during judicial proceedings, the said Judge or Magistrate cannot himself try and punish the offender, in view of the bar contained in Section 487 Cr.P.C. The proper course is for the concerned Judge or Magistrate to file or forward a complaint to the competent Magistrate, who shall then proceed in accordance with Sections 200 to 204 Cr.P.C and thereafter try the case in accordance with law. Thus, the learned Additional Sessions Judge had no jurisdiction to directly sentence the appellant under Section 174 PPC himself. A similar view has also been taken by this Court in Amjid Ali IHC v. The State (2024 PCr.LJ Peshawar 1803), wherein this Court held as follows:
“11. It is also not out of context to mention here that according to clauses (b) and (c) of subsection (1) of section 195 Cr.P.C. only the offences against public justice and those relating to documents given in evidence are to be prosecuted by the subordinate courts. Needless to mention that other offences of contempt of lawful authority of public servants under sections 172 to 188 P.P.C to be dealt with under the ordinary procedure laid down for the trial of other criminal cases. Thus, a line of distinction has been drawn between the cases of contempt relating to courts and public servants. For trials of the former a special procedure has been envisaged under Chapter XXXV of the Code of Criminal Procedure, however it must be kept into mind that the pre‐condition of written complaint by the authority concerned for offences of both types of contempt and perjury is common. Again, it is not to be over emphasized that the subordinate courts are required to adhere to the procedure prescribed in Chapter‐XXXV of Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 which contemplates special procedure in proceedings of offences affecting the administration of justice subject to pre‐condition contained in section 195 Cr.P.C. and modus operandi under this chapter is further divided in two categories…”
The crux of the above judgments is that offences falling within clauses (b) and (c) of Section 195(1) Cr.P.C are to be dealt with in accordance with the special procedure provided under Chapter XXXV Cr.P.C, comprising Sections 476 to 487 Cr.P.C, whereas offences falling under clause (a) remain subject to the mandatory condition prescribed in Section 195(1) Cr.P.C. In Muhammad Amar v. The State (1999 MLD 2023 Karachi), the Sindh High Court has also taken a similar view in the following words:
Keeping in view the facts and circumstances of this case as well as the provisions contained in clause (1)(a) of section 195 and clause (1) of section 476, Cr.P.C. I am of the view that these revisions fall under the exceptional circumstances enumerated hereinabove as the cognizance taken by the learned Sessions Judge, the impugned order and the proceedings under section 174, P.P.C. are all completely without jurisdiction. If she had come to the conclusion that the applicants had committed the alleged offence she should have sent a complaint to a competent Court for disposal according to law. (see also Ghulam Murtaza v. The State 1991 MLD (Lahore) 1759). However, the learned Sessions Judge would be at liberty to take further action in light of what has been stated hereinabove. Subject to these observations, in exercise of the I E inherent powers of this Court, the impugned order is set aside and the revisions stand disposed of accordingly.
In the said case, it has specifically been held that the proper course in respect of an offence under Section 174 PPC is to send a complaint to the competent Court for proceedings in accordance with law. The same procedure has been adopted by this Court in Tariq Khan, SHO v. The State (Cr.A. No. 398‐P/2025, decided on 28.10.2025), whereby the conviction under Section 174 PPC has been set aside on the strength of the bar contained in Section 195(1)(a) Cr.P.C, and the case has been remanded for trial in accordance with law. More recently, a larger Bench of this Court, in the landmark judgment titled Asif Ullah v. Government of KPK,1 held in the following words:
101. A core requirement is timely trial and disposal, before witness memory fades. Delay in justice is denial of justice. A persistent obstacle to effective justice is the non‐attendance of prosecution witnesses, which is one of the biggest causes of delay. Courts may resort to coercive measures including warrant of arrest, proceedings under section 87/88 CrPc, 74 salary attachment, CNIC blocking, or taking action under sections 174 and 186(2) PPC, where absence is deliberate, while video‐link recording may be operationalised through the Criminal Justice Coordination Committee to ensure timely evidence. The prosecution must proactively secure the attendance of its witnesses, and while mere non‐appearance does not automatically justify acquittal, deliberate avoidance may warrant recourse to coercive measures under section 186(2) PPC against prosecution. However, a significant impediment persists: under section 195 CrPC, offences falling within sections 172 to 187 PPC cannot be taken cognizance of by trial courts on their own motion and are further excluded from the ambit of section 476 CrPC. This statutory bar diminishes judicial capacity to enforce compliance where witnesses or public officials obstruct proceedings. To effectively curb such practices and strengthen accountability within the system, the relevant provisions require amendment so that trial courts may directly take cognizance of such offences and ensure meaningful enforcement of summons and judicial orders. Thus, this issue is left to the relevant forum i.e. provincial legislature. The larger Bench, while taking cognizance of the abovementioned legal position, further made the following recommendations in paragraphs No. 122:
“Such delays may attract penal accountability under sections 166(1), 175, and 186(2) PPC, which penalize disobedience of lawful directions, omission to produce required documents, and obstruction or falsification of investigations. However, section 195 CrPC bars cognizance of these offences, and section 476 CrPC does not currently empower trial courts to proceed. The Court recommends that sections 166 to 187 PPC be expressly included within the scope of section 476 CrPC, enabling trial courts to take cognizance and ensure effective enforcement of duties. However, it is again the prerogative of legislature to do legislation, for our duty is to identify the lacunas.”
The larger Bench has specifically issued directions to all Courts in paragraph No. 132, under the heading “Directive for Courts,” which is reproduced hereunder for reference:
65. Every trial Court may take cognizance of offences relating to false evidence, perjury, fabrication of evidence, forgery, or obstruction of justice arise during proceedings by invoking section 476 CrPC2 and try offences defined under Sections 175, 178, 179, 180, 193–196, 199, 200, 205–211, 228, 463, 471, 475, and 476 PPC, whereas for offences under Sections 172, 173, 174, 176, 177, and 181–187 PPC, a complaint must be filed by the concerned public servant or by the Court under section 195 CrPC. As to Section 166(2) PPC, the Magistrate may take cognizance under section 190 (1) (C) of CrPC and try the offence as section 166 PPC falls outside the domain of section 195 CrPC. Finally, the Bench recommended appropriate legislation to the Government in paragraph No. 132, under the heading “Directions and Recommendations for Respondents,” in the following words:
“Section 476 of the CrPC may be amended to include within its scope the offences mentioned in section 195, subsection (1), clause (a) of CrPC. It is further recommended that sections 166(1) and 166(2) of PPC be incorporated into this provision so that proceedings for these offences may also be initiated through the mechanism provided under section 476 CrPC.”
The net ratio of the above‐referred judgments of this Court is that, in view of the bar contained in Section 195(1)(a) Cr.P.C, offences falling under Sections 172 to 187 PPC, including Section 174 PPC, cannot be tried directly by the Trial Court itself. It is for this reason that a recommendation has been made to the Government for appropriate amendment in Section 476 Cr.P.C, so as to include within its ambit clause (a) of Section 195(1) Cr.P.C. Such amendment would bring offences under Sections 172 to 187 PPC within the scope of Section 476 Cr.P.C and would enable the concerned Court to directly try such offences. However, until such amendment is made, the existing legal position, as explained in the above discussion and supported by the cited judgments, remains that the Trial Court has no jurisdiction to itself try an offence under Section 174 PPC due to the bar contained in Section 195(1)(a) Cr.P.C. It is a settled principle of law that where a statute requires a thing to be done in a particular manner, it must be done in that manner and not otherwise. Thus, keeping in view the foregoing reasons, Question No. 1 is answered in the negative.

case: WP No. 8159-P of 2025 Muhammad Arshad Khanm vs The State through AG KP
remarks:
i. We, therefore, hold that remissions already earned by a prisoner are not acts of executive grace or indulgence; they are statutory earnings that crystallize into vested rights. To view them otherwise would be to allow the state to confiscate an accrued reward after the prisoner has irrevocably performed the act required to earn it.
ii. To allow Section 9(A)(1) to extend the incarceration of those who have already fulfilled the statutory requirements for their release would be to sanction detention without lawful authority, a result that this Court, in the exercise of its constitutional duty, cannot and will not permit.
iii. Undoubtedly, legislative wisdom commands judicial respect, and courts must exercise restraint before questioning policy choices adopted by the legislature. Yet constitutional adjudication occasionally requires an inquiry into whether the practical consequences of legislation have been evaluated with sufficient comprehensiveness, particularly where those consequences bear directly upon the treatment of individuals within the criminal justice system.
iv. Legislation, after all, is a human endeavour, and constitutional review exists precisely to ensure that statutory classifications remain consistent with the broader values of fairness, proportionality, equality, and rational governance.
v. For while the criminal law must undoubtedly protect society from harm, it must also preserve, wherever reasonably possible, the prospect that those who have transgressed its commands may one day return as reformed individuals capable of living peacefully amongst their fellow citizens.
vi. Under our constitutional framework, there exists a fundamental presumption in favour of the constitutionality of a statute, predicated on the premise that the Legislature correctly appreciates the needs of its people and that its laws are directed at manifest problems. However, as established in I.A. Sharwani (supra) and Saleem Raza (supra), this presumption is rebuttable. The presumption of validity must yield where a statute, upon its face, creates an arbitrary classification that hits only a particular class without any real or substantial distinction.

case: W.P No. 530-M of 2025 Dr. Raqib Khan Vs Federation of Pakistan & others
remarks:
The preliminary issue before Court was whether the constitutional petition was maintainable despite the availability of a statutory appeal before the Medical Tribunal. The petitioner argued that section 6(2) of the Medical Tribunal Act, 2020 referred only to repealed enactments and did not expressly include orders passed under the Pakistan Medical and Dental Council Act, 2022.
The Court examined section 8 of the General Clauses Act, 1897 and the doctrine of legislative continuity, holding that where a statute is repealed and re-enacted, references to the repealed law in another enactment are ordinarily construed as references to the successor law unless a contrary intention appears.
The Court held that although the Pakistan Medical and Dental Council Act, 2022 was not specifically mentioned in section 6(2) of the Medical Tribunal Act, 2020, the appellate jurisdiction of the Medical Tribunal continued to cover decisions made under the successor legislation. Consequently, an adequate and efficacious alternate remedy of appeal was available to the petitioner. Therefore the constitutional petition was not maintainable due to the existence of the statutory remedy before the Medical Tribunal.
Where a statute referred to in a remedial provision is repealed and replaced by a successor enactment governing the same subject matter, the reference must ordinarily be construed as extending to the successor law by virtue of section 8 of the General Clauses Act, 1897. Accordingly, statutory appellate remedies remain available notwithstanding the repeal and re-enactment of the underlying legislation.

case: W.P No. 1517-M of 2025 Dilawar Khan Vs The State
remarks: The police or any other executive authority cannot publicly brand citizens as criminals or members of criminal groups through official social media platforms without statutory authority, due process, and a determination by a competent court. Such conduct violates the constitutional guarantees of legality (Article 4), life and reputation (Article 9), fair trial and presumption of innocence (Article 10-A), and dignity and privacy (Article 14), and is liable to be declared unlawful under Article 199 of the Constitution.

case: Cr. Rev No. 120-P of 2026 Jalal Ahmad Vs Bilal etc
remarks:
i. A concession extracted through legitimate forensic skill cannot, by itself, justify a declaration of hostility. Were the law otherwise, every successful cross-examination would become a ground for invoking Article 150. The Court must therefore inquire whether the answer given represents a truthful acknowledgment elicited through proper questioning or whether it constitutes a conscious and unexplained departure from an earlier position.
ii. The power to permit the cross-examination of one\'s own witness is undoubtedly discretionary, but that discretion must be exercised upon objective considerations. The Court should ordinarily examine: (i) whether the witness has materially departed from a previous statement on a matter of significance; (ii) whether the departure appears deliberate, evasive, or calculated to assist one side of the litigation; (iii) whether the altered version has caused genuine surprise to the party calling the witness; (iv) whether the inconsistency is capable of innocent explanation; and (v) whether the interests of justice require further testing of the witness through questions ordinarily permissible in cross-examination.
iii. the doctrine of hostile witnesses is not a doctrine about procedural stages but a doctrine about the reliability of testimony. Hostility is determined not by the point at which it manifests itself but by the conduct through which it is revealed. A witness is not hostile because he weakens a case; he becomes hostile when he abandons the obligation of truth.
iv. Had the legislature intended to confine the power to examination-in-chief alone, it could easily have done so in express terms. Instead, the statutory emphasis is placed upon the satisfaction of the Court and not upon the stage of the proceedings. The interpretation that the Court is inclined to adopt also finds substantial support from Indian jurisprudence, which has examined the doctrine with considerable depth.
v. The jurisdiction to invoke Article 150 arises not because a particular stage of examination has been reached, but because the conduct of the witness has disclosed circumstances rendering the ordinary testing of credibility inadequate. Once such circumstances appear, whether during examination-in-chief, cross-examination, re-examination, or at any other point before the witness is discharged, the Court remains empowered to act. Any contrary interpretation would elevate procedural chronology above the administration of justice and would frustrate the very purpose for which the doctrine of hostile witnesses evolved.
vi. If a witness were permitted to support one version at one stage of the proceedings and thereafter consciously depart from it on a material aspect without any apprehension of legal consequence, the result would be to undermine public confidence in the judicial process itself.
vii. The declaration of hostility is not a substitute for accountability. It is merely an evidentiary mechanism designed to permit further testing of credibility. Where circumstances disclose that a witness has knowingly departed from the truth, willfully suppressed material facts, or consciously made statements inconsistent with what he knows to be true, the matter may travel beyond the confines of Article 150 and enter the domain of offences relating to false evidence.
viii. The consistent and principled application of those provisions serves an important public purpose. It discourages deliberate falsehood, reinforces respect for the oath administered by the Court, and ensures that witnesses do not proceed upon the mistaken belief that calculated departures from the truth are devoid of legal consequence.

case: Cr. A No. 424-P of 2025 Jehanzeb Vs The State etc
remarks:
i. At first glance, the matter may appear uncomplicated; however, a closer examination reveals a more nuanced legal question. The issue is whether the entirety of the FIR becomes inadmissible merely because the maker subsequently assumes the status of an accused, or whether those portions of the report which do not amount to a confession of guilt may still retain evidentiary relevance.
ii. Put differently, the Court is required to determine whether the inculpatory part of the report alone is liable to be excluded from consideration while the remaining portions, to the extent they contain relevant facts and information, may still be looked into for permissible purposes under the law.
iii. It is this delicate legal distinction that the Court is required to examine before evaluating the remaining prosecution evidence and determining whether the conviction recorded by the learned Trial Court can be sustained.
iv. We are, therefore, of the considered view that the portion of the FIR wherein the appellant purportedly admitted having caused the death of the deceased and disclosed the motive behind the occurrence is, in substance, nothing but a confession made before a police officer. Such a statement squarely attracts the mischief contemplated by Articles 38 and 39 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order and cannot be treated as substantive evidence against its maker.
v. The unexplained silence, the failure to take immediate steps, and the absence of any prompt disclosure, when evaluated in the backdrop of the entire evidentiary record, provide an additional link in the chain of circumstances which the Court is required to consider while arriving at a just conclusion in the matter.
vi. It is well-settled that the discovery of a fact pursuant to information supplied by an accused, to the extent it leads to recovery of a material object, is a relevant circumstance under the law of evidence. When these circumstances are examined in their totality, namely, the admitted matrimonial relationship, the undisputed ownership and control of the house, the recovery of the dead body from a concealed place within that house, the presence of blood-stained material and weapon of offence, and the conduct of the appellant in leading the police to the place of recovery, they collectively form a coherent chain of circumstances. While no single circumstance, in isolation, may be conclusive of guilt, their cumulative effect cannot be ignored.

case: CM No. 193-B of 2026 in WP No. 437-B of 2024 Dilawar Khan & 02 others v. Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa through Secretary Home Department & 02 others
remarks:
Miscellaneous Application
Held: Constitution of Pakistan, 1973---Article 199---Article 199(1A)---Code of Civil Procedure, 1908--- Section 2(2)---Order XLI, Rule 5(2)---Suspension of judgment---Constitutional jurisdiction--- Maintainability of application---Scope---Applicant, Director Anti-Corruption, sought suspension of judgment rendered in constitutional proceedings, whereby FIR registered by Anti-Corruption Establishment had been quashed and directions had been issued to place matter before competent authority for initiation of appropriate proceedings against concerned officers for abuse of authority and lack of bona fides in registration of a criminal case---Application was filed under Order XLI, Rule 5(2) CPC to enable applicant to avail further legal remedy in accordance with law---Applicant contended that directions contained in judgment offended Article 199(1A) of the Constitution and Section 5 of the West Pakistan Anti-Corruption Establishment Ordinance, 1961, and relied upon previous orders wherein operation of judgments had been suspended to facilitate recourse to the Hon’ble Supreme Court of Pakistan---Held, provisions of Order XLI, Rule 5(2) CPC apply to decrees passed in exercise of ordinary civil jurisdiction and cannot be invoked for suspension of judgments rendered by High Court in exercise of constitutional jurisdiction under Article 199 of the Constitution---Mere final adjudication of a matter by a constitutional Court does not transform its judgment into a “decree” within the meaning of Section 2(2) CPC---Judgments delivered under Article 199 derive their authority, nature and enforceability from the Constitution itself and stand on a distinct legal footing---Previous orders suspending judgments in other matters were passed in their own peculiar facts and statutory contexts and did not constitute a binding principle authorizing suspension of constitutional judgments as a matter of right---Directions contained in impugned judgment merely required placement of matter before competent authority for consideration and initiation of appropriate proceedings in accordance with law, and neither curtailed nor prejudiced jurisdiction of any statutory authority---In absence of any express provision of law, High Court could not suspend its own judgment rendered in constitutional jurisdiction for a specified or indefinite period merely to enable an aggrieved party to pursue further remedy-
--Application held to be devoid of merit and dismissed.

case: W.P No. 4949-P of 2023 Mian Zahir Shah Vs Raza Jan & others
remarks:
Section 13(6) of the West Pakistan Urban Rent Restriction Ordinance, 1959
A plain reading of Section 13(6) manifests that the Rent Controller is vested with authority to direct deposit of rent at the earliest stage of proceedings, even before framing of issues, where the relationship of landlord and tenant exists or stands admitted. It further leaves little room for discretion once the tenant defaults in compliance, as striking off defence and restoration of possession to the landlord follow as statutory consequences.
In the instant matter, the relationship of landlord and tenant was not merely inferential but expressly admitted by the respondent himself in his earlier civil suit, wherein in paragraph No.2 of the plaint he categorically acknowledged payment of rent by his predecessor as well as by himself to the predecessor-in-interest of the petitioners. Such admission constitutes substantive evidence and squarely attracts the doctrine of estoppel embodied in Article 115 of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984.
The contention subsequently raised by the respondent that the petitioners were not landlords is, therefore, wholly misconceived and self-destructive. A litigant cannot be permitted to assume inconsistent positions before different fora to suit his convenience. The respondent, having unequivocally admitted the tenancy relationship and having acted upon the same by payment of rent, could not later resiled therefrom merely on account of notices issued by the Pakhtunkhwa Highways Authority. The law does not permit a party to approbate and reprobate simultaneously, nor can one be allowed to blow hot and cold in the same breath.
Equally settled is the principle that compliance with an order passed under Section 13(6) of the Ordinance is mandatory in nature. Failure to deposit rent within prescribed time, even for a single day without legally justifiable explanation, ordinarily entails ejectment.

case: W.P No. 6436-P of 2026 Rafiq Khan and others Vs Federation of Pakistan through Federation Secretary and others
remarks: During the existence of an adequate alternate department remedy, a writ petition is ordinarily not maintainable. The cases where petitioners seek cancellation of Afghan Citizen Cards (ACC)/Proof of Registration (POR) Cards without first approaching the NADRA authorities and availing the prescribed department mechanism, such writ petitions shall not be maintainable before this Court.

case: F.A.O No. 14-P of 2022 Mst. Badam Zari Vs Z.T.B.L
remarks:
(a) Order XXI, Rule 89, C.P.C. – Scope and Independence of Remedy
Held- A plain reading of Order XXI, Rule 89 shows that it is a specific and independent statutory remedy available to a person interested in the property sold in execution. The right under this rule is not dependent upon the setting aside of the ex-parte decree. Nor can the application be rejected merely on the ground that some other provision might also have been available. The moment the sale has taken place and the applicant, being a person covered by the rule, deposits the amount contemplated by the rule, the Court is bound to examine the matter within the four corners of Rule 89 itself.
(b) Order XXI, Rule 89, C.P.C. read with Rule 92(2) – Unqualified Right and Judicial Discretion
Held- The true scope of Order XXI, Rule 89, when read in conjunction with Rule 92(2) of the same Order, establishes that an applicant who either owns the property or holds an interest therein has an unqualified right to have an auction sale set aside. This right is distinct and independent --because, unlike Rule 90 which requires proof of material irregularity or fraud, Rule 89 operates as a statutory indulgence or a “last chance” for the owner to save their property by simply satisfying the decree and compensating the purchaser. The mandatory language of Rule 92(2) is what renders this right unqualified; it provides that where the deposit required by Rule 89 is made within the statutory period, the Court “shall” make an order setting aside the sale. This leaves no room for judicial discretion or the evaluation of the legality of the sale process itself.
Ref: Zakaria Ghani v. Muhammad Ikhlaq Memon (PLD 2016 SC 229)
(c) Order XXI, Rule 89, C.P.C. – Purpose and Final Opportunity
Held- The provision is intended to defeat the claim of the auction purchaser unless the decree is simultaneously satisfied... Rule 89 confers a right even after the property is sold to satisfy the decree-holder’s claim while compensating the purchaser. Therefore, once the appellant in this case made the necessary deposit, the Court was bound to examine the matter strictly within the four corners of Rule 89. The right is unqualified because it allows the interested party to bypass the complexities of proving fraud or irregularity, provided they meet the strict 30-day limitation and deposit requirements. ...this is a final opportunity to get the sale set aside before confirmation.
Ref: Mst. Anwar Sultana v. Bank Al-Falah Ltd. (2014 SCMR 1222), Alhamdi Begum v. National Bank Of Pakistan Karachi (PLD 1976 Karachi 723).
(d) Order XXI, Rule 89 vs Rule 90 – Misconception of Scheme
Held- The said stance proceeds on a misconception of the scheme of the Code. The present matter is not one where the appellant was seeking to question the decree simpliciter; rather, the appellant was invoking the statutory remedy available against an execution sale by making the deposit required by law. Once the sale had already been effected and the deposit was made in accordance with Rule 89, the respondents could not validly defeat the application by insisting upon a different provision.
(e) Order XXI, Rule 89, C.P.C. – Statutory Command vs. Equity
Held- Those aspects, though relevant in a challenge under other provisions, cannot override the express statutory command of Rule 89 once the appellant has complied with its mandatory conditions. The auction purchaser’s interest, though undoubtedly deserving of protection in ordinary circumstances, cannot prevail against a valid statutory application made under the rule. The law on judicial sales is settled that while sanctity of auction proceedings must ordinarily be preserved, where the legislature itself has provided a redeeming provision on deposit, the Court cannot deny relief on equitable impressions alone.
Appeal allowed.

case: Cr.A No. 1129-P of 2025 M/s Kundi Paper Mills Vs Enviromental Protection Agency, KP Peshawar.
remarks:
So far as the contention of counsel for the appellant that the documents exhibited in evidence before the Tribunal are photocopies, therefore, being secondary evidence, the same is inadmissible in evidence under the provisions of the Qanun‐e‐Shahadat Order, 1984, is concerned, the learned Tribunal in its judgement has commented on this point by observing that:
“The points of learned defense counsel that
……………..
…………….
having no force because no trial could be conducted without production of original record and where ever doubt arises the court was at liberty to check the same.”.
Even otherwise, mere production of secondary evidence does not automatically render the entire prosecution case unreliable. The Court is required to examine whether any prejudice was caused to the accused and whether the remaining evidence independently established the commission of the offence. As stated above, the officials of the Agency who collected the waste water samples appeared before the Tribunal and fully described the manner in which the samples were obtained and transmitted to the laboratory. Their testimony remained consistent on all material particulars. Nothing important could be brought about during cross‐examination to suggest that the sampling process was manipulated or that the samples did not belong to the appellant Mill. The defense did not dispute the ownership or operation of the Mill nor did it produce any different laboratory report to challenge the lab reports reflected in the photocopies. Even though objection was raised to the exhibition of the photocopies, yet the defense did not seek production of the original documents, nor did it show that the photocopies were not the accurate copies of the original reports. The objection, therefore, remained technical in nature. It is settled principle that procedural or technical irregularities which do not cause failure of justice are not sufficient to reverse a conviction which is otherwise based on reliable evidence. It is worth mentioning that the present case is regarding an environmental offence and where the evidence as a whole satisfactorily establishes environmental violation and the genuineness of the laboratory reports is corroborated by oral testimony and surrounding circumstances, the conviction is not liable to be set aside only because photocopies of the reports were exhibited. Though primary evidence is generally preferred, yet the law does not permit acquittal solely on account of an irregularity in proof when the truth of the contents of the documents stands independently established through consistent testimony of witnesses and other corroborative material available on record.

case: C.R No. 245-P of 2025 Shahid Ali Khan Vs Olas Khan etc
remarks:
Section 372 & 381 od Succession Act 1925.
Succession Act, 1925 provides a special and summary procedure for adjudication of applications seeking issuance of a succession certificate in respect of the debts and securities of a deceased person. While Section 372 regulates the institution of such proceedings, Section 381 of the Act, 1925 delineates the legal effect of a certificate so issued. The underlying object of the statutory scheme is to facilitate expeditious realization of debts and securities belonging to the estate of the deceased without embroiling the parties in protracted disputes concerning title or succession. A succession certificate, however, neither adjudicates the proprietary rights of the persons claiming interest in the estate nor conclusively determines their status as legal heirs or their respective shares in the recoverable assets. Its issuance is summary in character and confined to authorizing the certificate-holder to collect debts and realize securities due to the deceased. The legislative rationale behind such certificate is primarily to indemnify and protect the debtor or obligor making payment in good faith to the holder thereof, so that such payment may operate as a valid discharge against future claims. Nonetheless, the holder of the certificate does not acquire absolute ownership or exclusive title over the amount so recovered; rather, he remains under a legal obligation to account for and distribute the realized amount amongst all persons lawfully entitled thereto in accordance with their respective legal shares and rights. Thus, a certificate issued under the Succession Act, 1925 does not confer title, ownership, or final declaration of succession upon the grantee, but merely furnishes an enabling authority to recover debts and securities on behalf of the estate of the deceased.
It is a settled principle of law that any debt, liability, or lawful claim alleged to be recoverable from a deceased person does not automatically become enforceable against his legal heirs merely by reason of succession. Such liability can only be enforced against the legal representatives to the extent of the estate inherited by them and subject to a lawful adjudication by a competent Court of law. Before fastening liability upon the legal heirs, the existence, nature, and quantum of the claim must first be judicially determined through appropriate proceedings, wherein the alleged debt is established in accordance with law. A Court exercising jurisdiction in succession proceedings under the Succession Act, 1925 is not vested with authority to adjudicate disputed claims of debt, determine contested liabilities, or secure alleged recoveries in favour of an objector whose claim remains unsubstantiated and has not been crystallized through an independent suit or competent adjudication. The jurisdiction of the Succession Court is summary and limited in nature, confined merely to determining entitlement for the issuance of a succession certificate in respect of debts and securities of the deceased, and does not extend to deciding complicated questions relating to liability or recovery.

case: Cr.A No. 308-P of 2025 Usman Vs The State & another
remarks:
i. It is a matter of serious reflection that societies, even when grounded in deep religious traditions, sometimes witness a gradual distancing from the very moral and spiritual foundations that once defined their collective conscience. This departure is not always abrupt, rather, it is often incremental, shaped by ignorance, misguidance, superstition, and a weakening of the connection between belief and conduct.
ii. It is not the function of a court of law to convict on the basis of emotion, public sentiment, religious outrage, or philosophical reflection. Nor is it the purpose of these introductory observations to suggest that the accused must necessarily be guilty of the allegations levelled against them. Indeed, one of the foundational principles of criminal jurisprudence is that every accused person enters the courtroom clothed with the presumption of innocence. It is not the accusation but the evidence that determines criminal liability. No individual can be condemned merely because the allegations against him appear grave, offensive, or morally disturbing. The law requires proof, and proof alone.
iii. Experience demonstrates that allegations of a serious nature are rarely fabricated without some corresponding reason, whether born of vengeance, personal gain, or longstanding hostility. In the present case, however, no such motive has surfaced from the record.
iv. The law does not require that every circumstance, viewed in isolation, should conclusively establish guilt. What it demands is that the cumulative effect of the evidence be such as to inspire confidence and exclude reasonable doubt.
v. Liability is thus frequently assessed through the prism of overt conduct, making the distinction between the principal actor and a mere spectator comparatively straightforward. The present case stands on a markedly different footing. The prosecution does not allege a crime born of sudden impulse, personal animosity, or an unforeseen chain of events. Rather, the occurrence is projected as the execution of a preconceived design intended to achieve a particular objective. The significance of such an occurrence cannot, therefore, be measured solely by identifying the individual who performed each physical act. Equally relevant are the collective conduct of the participants, their awareness of the purpose being pursued, and the degree to which each associated himself with the accomplishment of that purpose. For this reason, the Court considers it unsafe to evaluate the role of the appellant through standards ordinarily employed in more conventional criminal cases. Human participation in unlawful conduct does not always manifest itself through visible action.
vi. There are situations in which criminal responsibility arises not merely from what a person does, but from what he knowingly joins, supports, facilitates, or allows to be accomplished through his conscious association with another. The law is concerned not only with the hand that executes an act but also with the mind that aligns itself with its execution.

case: W.P No. 401-P of 2026 with C.M No. 14-P of 2026 Muhammad Umair Vs Inspector General of Police, KP at Central Police Office (CPO), Peshawar & another.
remarks:
Constitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973—Article 199—The doctrine of Legitimate Expectation—— Held —The doctrine of legitimate expectation connotes that a person may have a reasonable expectation of being treated in a certain way by administrative authorities owing to some uniform practice or an explicit promise made by the concerned authority. In fact, a legitimate expectation ascends in consequence of a promise, assurance, practice or policy made, adopted or announced by or on behalf of government or a public authority. When such a legitimate expectation is obliterated, it affords a locus standi to challenge the administrative action and even, in the absence of a substantive right, a legitimate expectation may allow an individual to seek judicial review of a wrongdoing; and in deciding whether the expectation was legitimate or not, the Court may consider that the decision of the public authority has breached a legitimate expectation and, if it is proved, then the Court may annul the decision and direct the concerned authority/person to live up to the legitimate expectation. This doctrine is basically applied as a tool to watch over the actions of administrative authorities and in essence imposes obligations on all public authorities to act fair and square in all matters encompassing legitimate expectation.
The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Initial Appointment to Civil Posts (Relaxation of Upper Age Limit) Rules, 2008—Rule 3, 5, 6—Doctrine of Legitimate Expectation— Held— Once a candidate is allowed to participate in the entire selection process without any objection to his eligibility, the employer cannot, at a later stage, raise an objection regarding the same eligibility criteria that were either known or ought to have been known. The respondents’ conduct—permitting the petitioner to sit for the ETEA test, qualify it on merit, appear in the interview, secure recommendation by the Selection Committee, and ultimately receive a provisional offer of appointment—unequivocally indicates an implied acceptance of his entitlement to age relaxation as per the advertised terms and existing rules. This conduct created a legitimate expectation in the petitioner that he would be appointed, subject only to successful completion of the remaining formalities. The doctrine of legitimate expectation is an equitable principle evolved by the Courts for doing justice and there is no reason why it should be given only a limited application by way of defence.
Statutory Rules & Policy Decisions—Prospective vs. Retrospective application—Provincial Cabinet Decision dated 15.01.2025— Held— It is a fundamental principle of law that statutory rules, notifications, or policy decisions generally operate prospectively unless expressly stated otherwise or if their retrospective application is necessitated by clear legislative intent and is for public good, without adversely affecting vested rights. Applying a subsequent policy decision retrospectively to deny a right that had already accrued or was in the process of accrual would be unjust and unlawful. The ban imposed subsequent to the selection could not take away the rights of the petitioner which has already accrued to her and was also finalized. The Provincial Cabinet Decision of 15.01.2025, banning age relaxation, being prospective in nature, cannot be applied to the petitioner’s case, which commenced under the regime of the 2008 Rules and the terms of the advertisement.
Doctrine of Locus Poenitentiae—Administrative Orders— Held— Where an administrative order has taken effect and valuable rights have accrued therefrom, the authority’s power to withdraw or rescind such order becomes significantly restricted. The doctrine of locus poenitentiae cannot be invoked indefinitely to undo completed administrative actions, particularly where no fraud, concealment, misrepresentation, or misconduct is attributable to the beneficiary. Similarly, the petitioner cannot also be made to suffer for an omission or oversight on the part of public functionaries. The well-established maxim that “an act or omission of a public authority should prejudice none” squarely applies.
Ref: National Database and Registration Authority (NADRA) v. Jawad Khan (2023 SCMR 1381); Uzma Manzoor v. Vice-Chancellor, Khushal Khan Khattak University (2022 SCMR 694); Rukhsar Ali v. Government of N.W.F.P (2003 PLC 1453); Commissioner Inland Revenue and others v. Mekotex (Pvt.) Limited and others (PLD 2024 SC 1168); Fatima and others v. Government of Pakistan (2018 PLC 292); Muhammad Yasin v. Secretary, Government of Punjab (2008 PLC(CS) 303); Shahbaz v. Crown (PLD 1956 F.C. 46); Pakistan v. Muhammad Himayatullah (PLD 1969 SC 407).
Ref: Halsbury’s Laws of England, 3rd Edition Vol. II. (AIR 1965 S.C. 491). Petition Allowed.

case: C.R No. 1431-P of 2025 Muhammad Iqbal Vs Abdul Khaliq & another
remarks:
Parentage dispute: Article 46 & 64(5) of Qanun-e-Shahadat Order 1984.
o Courts of law are guardians not only of legal rights but also of the dignity, security, and welfare of minors. Children must never be converted into instruments for settling personal vendettas or family rivalries. To permit the respondent, after allowing the child to remain with the petitioner throughout her formative years, to reopen the issue of her parentage merely because of subsequent hostility between two brothers would be to sacrifice the welfare of the child at the altar of personal acrimony. Such an approach would expose the minor to lasting psychological scars, undermine her emotional security, disrupt her social identity, and irreparably impair her future development. Equity, good conscience, and the doctrine of acquiescence equally militate against entertaining such a destabilizing claim after the lapse of more than a decade.
o The cumulative effect of the above statutory provisions and judicial pronouncements is that, in disputes relating to parentage, official records such as birth registers, birth certificates, Child Registration Certificates, National Identity Cards, and other contemporaneously prepared public documents constitute the most reliable category of evidence. Once such documentary evidence consistently records the parentage of a child, the burden shifts heavily upon the party challenging those entries to rebut them by evidence of equal or superior probative value. Mere oral assertions, belated claims, or family disputes surfacing long after the creation of official records do not possess sufficient evidentiary weight to displace the statutory presumption of correctness attached to public documents maintained in the ordinary course of official business. Although the plaintiff/respondent has challenged the authenticity and correctness of the entire documentary record, he has failed to produce any cogent, convincing, credible, or unimpeachable evidence capable of rebutting the presumption attached to those public documents. In the absence of evidence of equal or superior probative value, the official records continue to hold the field, and the respondent`s bare oral assertions are wholly insufficient in law to invalidate or override the consistent documentary evidence on record.
o In addition, under Article 46(5) of the Qanun-e-Shahadat Order, 1984, statements relating to the existence of a relationship by blood, marriage, or adoption are relevant provided they are made by a person having special means of knowledge before any dispute concerning such relationship has arisen. Likewise, Article 64, Illustration (b), recognizes that where a person has consistently been treated by members of the family as the legitimate child of another, such conduct constitutes a relevant circumstance for determining questions of parentage. These provisions underscore the legislative policy that, in disputes concerning status and parentage, contemporaneous declarations, family conduct, and long-standing recognition assume considerable evidentiary significance. The documentary evidence assumes even greater importance where it emanates from official records maintained by public authorities in the ordinary course of their statutory functions.

case: W.P No. 518-A of 2025 Hazir Khan and others Vs Government of Khyber Pakhtunkhwa
remarks:
(a) Section 44 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Local Government Act, 2013 establishes a complete statutory framework for the levy and recovery of Urban Immovable Property Tax. While every tehsil is deemed to be a rating area, the imposition of property tax is not automatic. Section 44(2) expressly requires that the applicable rate of tax be determined and approved by the respective Tehsil Council. The proviso further provides that where no rate has been determined, the concerned area shall be deemed exempt from property tax until such determination is made. Section 44(3) merely preserves continuity of rates that were already lawfully operative and cannot be interpreted in a manner that nullifies the requirement of approval by the Tehsil Council or the statutory exemption created by the proviso.
The provision must be read as a cohesive whole. The declaration of a tehsil as a rating area only identifies the territorial scope of taxation; it does not by itself create an enforceable tax liability. Unless the rate is lawfully determined and approved by the competent authority, no valid assessment, demand, or recovery of Urban Immovable Property Tax can be sustained. Any contrary interpretation would render the proviso and the role assigned to the Tehsil Council redundant, contrary to settled principles of statutory interpretation.
(b) The power to determine and approve property tax rates under Sections 42 and 44 is not a purely administrative function. Although local governments do not possess plenary legislative powers comparable to Parliament or Provincial Assemblies, they may exercise delegated or quasi-legislative powers expressly conferred by statute. The determination of tax rates directly affects the proprietary and financial rights of citizens and is therefore legislative or, at the very least, quasi-legislative in character.
The statutory scheme entrusts this authority to elected local councils because taxation decisions involve public accountability, democratic representation, and fiscal policy considerations. Section 42(2) further reinforces this position by requiring prior publication of proposals, consideration of objections, and approval by the local council before any tax can be levied. These safeguards are substantive requirements intended to ensure transparency, participation, and lawful exercise of taxing powers. Consequently, the determination or approval of tax rates cannot be treated as a routine administrative act capable of being exercised by executive functionaries.
(c) The contention that administrative officers could exercise the powers of Tehsil Councils under Section 120-A of the Act was rejected. Section 120-A was enacted as a transitional provision to ensure continuity of local administration during periods when elected local governments were not functioning. Its purpose was limited to facilitating day-to-day administration and maintaining local governance during the transitional period. The power to levy, determine, or approve taxes is fundamentally legislative and financial in nature and cannot be assumed by administrative officers in the absence of clear and express statutory authorization. Interpreting Section 120-A as transferring such powers to executive officers would undermine the constitutional scheme of democratic local government and render the statutory role of elected councils ineffective. Administrative officers could therefore perform routine administrative functions but could not lawfully exercise legislative or fiscal powers specifically reserved for representative local institutions.
Accordingly, although every tehsil constituted a rating area under Section 44(1), the levy, assessment, and recovery of Urban Immovable Property Tax remained subject to strict compliance with Sections 42 and 44 of the Act. Since the determination and approval of tax rates had not been carried out by the competent Tehsil Councils, all notifications, approvals, assessments, demand notices, recovery notices, and related proceedings issued by administrative officers were held to be without lawful authority and were set aside. Nevertheless, the authorities were permitted to initiate fresh proceedings in accordance with law after complying with all mandatory statutory requirements.

case: W.P No. 187-B of 2026 Syed Muhammad Vs Khan Bahadur alias Fazal-ur-Rehman, etc
remarks:
Writ Petition
Held: Constitution of Pakistan, 1973---Article 199---Code of Civil Procedure, 1908---Sections 16 & 20--- Order VII, Rule 10---Territorial jurisdiction---Return of plaint---Dispute relating to immovable property--- Determination of rights and interests in property---Scope---Petitioner instituted a civil suit before the Civil Court at Bannu, seeking declaration, cancellation of transactions and permanent mandatory injunction in respect of Plot No.1779 situated in Jalozai Housing Scheme at Tehsil Pabbi, District Nowshera, alleging that the property had been fraudulently transferred without his consent through arbitration proceedings conducted by the respondents---Defendant questioned maintainability of suit on the ground that the property was situated in District Nowshera, the transfer transaction had taken place at Peshawar, and the record was maintained by the Provincial Housing Authority at Peshawar---Trial Court returned the plaint under Order VII, Rule 10 CPC for presentation before the Court of competent jurisdiction, which order was maintained in revision---Petitioner contended that the sale deed, payment of consideration, and arbitration proceedings culminating in fraudulent transfer of the suit plot had taken place at Bannu and, therefore, part of the cause of action had arisen within the territorial jurisdiction of Trial Court at Bannu---Held, a conjoint reading of Sections 16 and 20 CPC reveals that where the dispute pertains to determination of rights or interests in immovable property, the special provisions of Section 16 CPC would govern the matter and jurisdiction is to be determined with reference to the situs of the property, while Section 20 CPC, being a general provision, cannot override the mandate of Section 16 CPC---Record revealed that contesting defendant resided at Peshawar, while the Provincial Housing Authority, which maintained the official record pertaining to the allotment and transfer of plots, including the disputed plot, was situated at Peshawar, whereas the suit property was situated in Jalozai Housing Scheme at Tehsil Pabbi, District Nowshera---Ancillary facts, such as the alleged execution of documents, payment of consideration, or conduct of arbitration proceedings, do not confer territorial jurisdiction upon the Trial Court at Bannu when the principal controversy relates to immovable property situated beyond its territorial limits---Only the Court within whose local jurisdiction the property is situated is competent to entertain and adjudicate such dispute---Alleged fraudulent acts of one of the defendants residing at Bannu do not create a cause of action under Section 20(c) CPC so as to confer jurisdiction upon the Civil Court at Bannu---Findings recorded by the Courts below were in accordance with settled principles of law and suffered from no jurisdictional defect, illegality or material irregularity warranting interference in constitutional jurisdiction---Petition dismissed in limine with liberty to the petitioner to present the plaint before the Court of competent jurisdiction at District Peshawar or District Nowshera, as the case may be, in accordance with law.
